texto: It was getting dark by the time I went out, and nobody who knows the country will need to be told how black is the darkness of a November night under high laurel bushes and yew-trees. I walked into the heart of the shrubberies two or three times, not seeing a step before me, till I came out upon the broader carriage-road, where the trees opened a little, and there was a faint grey glimmer of sky visible, under which the great limes and elms stood darkling like ghosts; but it grew black again as I approached the corner where the ruins lay. ("The Open Door")
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Mrs. Oliphant: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. O trecho “It was getting dark by the time I went out, and nobody who knows the country will need to be told…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que amor, countryside, dark pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de reflexao-noturna e boa-noite.

texto: He was highly spoken of, everybody knew; but nobody knew who had spoken highly of him…

texto: There is a great deal in choosing colours that go well with one's complexion. People think of that of that for their dresses, but not for their rooms., which are of so much more importance. I should have liked blue, but blue gets so soon tawdry. I think, (...) that I have enough complexion at present to venture upon a pale spring green.

texto: As she stepped into the steamboat at Dover which was to convey her to scenes so new, Lucilla felt more and more that she who held the reorganisation of society in Carlingford in her hands was a woman with a mission.

texto: Then there rose up before her a vision of a parish saved, a village reformed, a county reorganised, and a triumphant election at the end, the recompense and crown of all, which should put the government of the country itself, to a certain extent, into competent hands.

texto: Dear Mrs Chiley," said Lucilla, "it doesn't matter in the least what you wear; there are only to be gentlemen, you, know, and one never dresses for gentlemen. (...) Their vanity is something dreadful-but it is one of my principles never to dress unless there are ladies.

texto: Oh, never mind the fashion. When one has a style of one's own, it is always twenty times better.
